data.title: Texas Instruments signals 70% data center growth as industrial, automotive, and data center reach 75% of 2025 revenue
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Texas Instruments (TXN) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Haviv Ilan reported, "Revenue came in about as expected at $4.4 billion, a decrease of 7% sequentially and an increase of 10% from the same quarter a year ago." Ilan highlighted that analog revenue grew 14% year-over-year, embedded processing grew 8%, and the Other segment declined from the year-ago quarter.
  • Ilan explained a reorganization of end markets to include data center, now grouping industrial, automotive, data center, personal electronics, and communications equipment as key sectors. He stated, "Our end markets are now industrial, automotive, data center, personal electronics and communications equipment."
  • Ilan disclosed, "Industrial was $5.8 billion, up 12% year-on-year and was 33% of revenue. Automotive was $5.8 billion, up 6% year-on-year and was 33% of revenue. Data center was $1.5 billion, up 64% year-on-year and was 9% of revenue. Personal electronics was $3.7 billion, up 7% year-on-year and was 21% of revenue. Communications equipment was about $500 million, up about 20% year-on-year and was 3% of revenue."
  • Ilan emphasized, "Industrial, automotive and data center combined made up about 75% of TI's revenue in 2025, up from about 43% in 2013."
  • CFO Rafael Lizardi stated, "Gross profit in the quarter was $2.5 billion or 56% of revenue. Sequentially, gross profit margin decreased 150 basis points. Operating profit was $1.5 billion in the quarter or 33% of revenue and was up 7% from the year-ago quarter."
  • Lizardi added, "Net income in the fourth quarter was $1.2 billion or $1.27 per share. Earnings per share included a $0.06 reduction not in our original guidance related to the noncash impairment of goodwill in our Other segment and other tax-related items."

Outlook

  • Lizardi provided guidance: "We expect TI revenue in the range of $4.32 billion to $4.68 billion and earnings per share to be in the range of $1.22 to $1.48."
  • Lizardi stated, "We continue to expect our effective tax rate for 2026 to be about 13% to 14%."
  • Ilan noted, "I expect this market [data center] to continue to grow in Q1. It's been growing for now 7 quarters in a row for us."

Financial Results

  • Lizardi reported, "Cash flow from operations was $2.3 billion in the quarter. Capital expenditures were $925 million in the quarter. In the quarter, we paid $1.3 billion in dividends and repurchased $403 million of our stock."
  • Lizardi highlighted, "We also increased our dividend per share by 4% in the fourth quarter to $1.42 per share, marking our 22nd consecutive year of dividend increases."
  • Lizardi stated, "Our balance sheet remains strong with $4.9 billion of cash and short-term investments at the end of the fourth quarter. Total debt outstanding was $14 billion with a weighted average coupon of 4%. Inventory at the end of the quarter was $4.8 billion, down $25 million from the prior quarter and days were 222, up 7 days sequentially."
  • For the full year, Lizardi said, "Cash flow from operations was $7.2 billion and capital expenditures were $4.6 billion. Free cash flow for 2025 was $2.9 billion or 17% of revenue, representing an increase of 96% from 2024."

Q&A

  • Ross Seymore, Deutsche Bank: Asked if anything unique was driving the above-seasonal Q1 guide. Ilan responded, "We did see orders improving throughout the quarter. And what guides our guidance is the stronger bookings."
  • Seymore followed up on gross margin implications. Lizardi answered, "Assume OpEx is up low single digits, and you should get into a reasonable number for gross margin. And the loadings will depend on demand, and we'll adjust those as needed."
  • James Schneider, Goldman Sachs: Asked about inventory levels and future plans. Ilan responded, "We are very pleased with the inventory position we have built. We are very proud of how we got there."
  • Schneider asked about prospects in personal electronics and communications. Ilan said, "Personal electronics...did grow for the year...We just saw a little bit of a weak Q4, I would say, below typical seasonality."
  • Harlan Sur, JPMorgan: Inquired about customer hesitancy in industrial. Ilan responded, "I do believe that the secular growth continues in industrial. We are looking at end equipment and generation to generation, we see just more content growth per system."
  • Sur also asked about fab build-outs and CapEx. Ilan reported, "We are very pleased about the execution in Sherman. It's actually ramped ahead of schedule, high yields."
  • Vivek Arya, BofA: Asked if Q1 strength was pricing related. Ilan said, "The answer is no, it's not pricing related."
  • Timothy Arcuri, UBS: Asked about CapEx trajectory. Lizardi replied, "We continue to expect CapEx for 2026 between $2 billion and $3 billion."
  • Thomas O'Malley, Barclays: Asked about auto market trends. Ilan stated, "We did see strength in the automotive in the second half of this year...I think secular growth continues into the foreseeable future."
  • Joshua Buchalter, TD Cowen: Asked about data center growth. Ilan responded, "Data center has grown nicely...most of our business is based on the analog side...I see the opportunity as an attractive one."
  • William Stein, Truist: Asked about bookings strength and backlog duration. Beckman explained, "We did see throughout the quarter backlog did build...we have seen our turns business also exhibit strength over the last several quarters."
  • Christopher Caso, Wolfe Research: Asked about factory loadings. Lizardi stated, "We are not making any disclosure right now on which way the loadings are going...there's nothing significant versus where we've been running in fourth quarter."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on Q1 guidance strength, gross margin trajectory, inventory management, and end-market recovery. The tone was cautiously optimistic with repeated probing for drivers behind above-seasonal guidance and market-specific recovery, and some skepticism regarding sustainability of demand.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing execution and strategic positioning. During Q&A, management was generally open but occasionally deflected specifics, especially regarding future loadings or pricing, using phrases such as "we are very pleased," "we'll adjust those as needed," and "we are in a very good position."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone shifted from concern about slow recovery and inventory build-up to cautious optimism about order momentum and data center growth. Management's confidence increased, reflecting improved bookings and operational progress.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4 guidance was notably above typical seasonality, with management citing stronger bookings and data center growth, compared to the previous quarter's moderate recovery outlook and flat-to-down seasonal comments.
  • Strategic focus evolved, with Q4 highlighting a new end-market structure and increased reporting transparency around data center, industrial, and automotive segments.
  • Analyst focus shifted from concerns about inventory and gross margin headwinds to questions about drivers of revenue upside, sustainability of growth in data center, and CapEx trajectory.
  • Management provided more specific forward-looking statements on CapEx, depreciation, and secular content growth, compared to more reserved language in Q3.
  • Key metrics such as free cash flow and revenue diversification improved, and management expressed greater confidence in operational readiness and end-market growth drivers.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged market volatility, with Ilan noting, "The market has been jittery in the last 12 years -- 12 months, sorry. And we'll just have to see how it plays out."
  • Concerns about pricing were addressed, with Ilan stating, "We have 80,000 products. Prices always go up and down. But for the company, the overall price effect like-for-like in '25, we expected it to be low single digits down."
  • Analyst questions highlighted potential risks from memory pricing and demand sustainability, especially in consumer and automotive sectors, but management reported no immediate negative impact.
  • Management reiterated flexibility in adjusting factory loadings and inventory to respond to shifts in demand.

Final Takeaway

Texas Instruments management emphasized that industrial, automotive, and data center now account for three-quarters of revenue, with data center growing 70% year-on-year and contributing significantly to above-seasonal Q1 guidance. Robust operational execution, disciplined capital allocation, and a strong inventory and capacity position support confidence in meeting customer demand and further capitalizing on long-term secular growth trends across key end markets.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Hanmi outlines low to mid-single-digit loan growth and deposit expansion strategy for 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Hanmi Financial Corporation (HAFC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • President and CEO Bonita Lee stated, "Our team delivered a solid performance in the fourth quarter, capping a strong year of growth for Hanmi. We believe we executed well on our priorities and advanced key initiatives we laid out at the start of the year. Specifically, we further enhanced the diversification of our loan portfolio and achieved mid-single-digit loan growth guidance." Lee also noted, "We managed the deposit cost and generated net interest margin expansion throughout 2025. Our noninterest-bearing deposits continue to represent 30% of total deposits, a tribute to the stability of our customer base."

  • CEO Lee emphasized, "Net income for the fourth quarter was $21.2 million or $0.70 per diluted share, down 3.7% due to lower noninterest income. However, net interest income increased to 2.9% and net interest margin expanded by 6 basis points to 3.28% from the prior quarter, reflecting a lower cost of funds and higher average loan balances."

  • CFO Romolo Santarosa reported, "For the fourth quarter, net interest income grew 2.9% from the previous quarter to $62.9 million as the average rate on interest-bearing deposits declined 20 basis points, while the average yield on loans declined by only 9 basis points and the average balance of loans increased 2.4%." Santarosa also noted, "Credit loss expense declined to $1.9 million as asset quality continued to be favorable with low net charge-offs to loans of 10 basis points, delinquent loans to loans at 0.27%, criticized loans to loans at 1.48% and nonperforming assets to total assets of 0.26%."

Outlook

  • CEO Lee outlined top priorities for 2026: "We expect to generate low to mid-single-digit loan growth with a continued emphasis on further diversifying the portfolio. Second, we are focused on growing deposits to support loan growth while maintaining a stable, well-balanced funding mix." Lee added, "We intend to sustain our commitment to disciplined expense management while we are investing selectively in talent and technology to support our long-term growth."

  • Management reaffirmed a focus on maintaining strong asset quality, with Lee stating, "Conservative underwriting standards, active portfolio monitoring and robust risk analysis remain foundational to how we operate and will continue to guide our decision-making as the economic environment evolves."

Financial Results

  • Hanmi reported net income for the fourth quarter of $21.2 million or $0.70 per diluted share. Net interest income for the quarter was $62.9 million, and the net interest margin expanded to 3.28%.

  • For the full year 2025, net income reached $76.1 million or $2.51 per diluted share. The company achieved loan growth of $312 million or 5% for the year. Noninterest income increased 7.6%, driven by a 39% increase in SBA loans sold.

  • Noninterest expenses for the quarter were $39.1 million, with an efficiency ratio of 54.95%. Tangible common equity per share increased 2.5% to $26.27 at year-end.

Q&A

  • Matthew Clark, Piper Sandler: Asked about a hospitality credit downgraded to special mention. CEO Lee responded, "As a part of our periodic review, we decided to place this particular loan in special mention category. It is a seasoned loan with a very strong sponsor with high liquidity...the property is going through a property improvement plan." Clark also inquired about expense outlook, with Ben Brodkowitz stating, "I think middle single digits is probably the right expectation over a 12-month scenario."

  • Clark asked about the CD repricing schedule. Chief Banking Officer Anthony Kim explained, "A little over $900 million cities are rolling off in first half at 4.01% and then followed by another little less than $900 million maturing in second quarter with a weighted average of 3.95%."

  • Clark questioned the buyback strategy. Brodkowitz responded, "The Board evaluates the capital return each quarter...we started to see share prices well above our tangible book."

  • Gary Tenner, D.A. Davidson: Asked about CD pricing power and competition. Kim noted, "Our CD retention rate has been around 90%, and we chose not to retain some of the CDs at irrational rates. So our CD retention rate went down to 80%." Lee added, "Within our corridor, there are still some of the banks that are actually running CD promotions above 3.85%."

  • Kelly Motta, KBW: Asked about the expense cadence and yields on new production. Kim explained, "We are being very selective in our loan originations, prioritizing our returns."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised detailed questions on credit quality, expense trajectory, deposit pricing, and capital return, with a neutral to slightly cautious tone, especially regarding competitive dynamics and expense run-rates.

  • Management maintained a confident posture in prepared remarks, emphasizing strategy execution and asset quality. In Q&A, responses were measured and occasionally defensive, particularly on credit downgrades and expense spikes, as seen when Lee stated, "We don't foresee any loss probabilities on this credit...it is due to our proactive monitoring process."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone was slightly more cautious, particularly regarding expense management and competitive deposit pricing, while analysts maintained a similar level of scrutiny.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management’s guidance for loan growth in 2026 shifted to specifically "low to mid-single-digit" from a more general "mid-single-digit" range in the prior quarter.

  • Expense management became a more pronounced discussion, with noninterest expenses rising in Q4 due to specific items, whereas Q3 highlighted record efficiency.

  • Deposit dynamics shifted, with a slight decrease in Q4, driven by competitive pressures and conscious decisions not to retain higher-cost CDs, contrasting with modest deposit growth in Q3.

  • Asset quality discussion remained strong both quarters, but Q4 included a specific mention of a hospitality loan downgrade, reflecting proactive credit monitoring.

  • Analysts’ focus shifted from optimism about growth drivers in Q3 to more questions about sustainability of expense trends and deposit pricing in Q4.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted competitive deposit pricing as a challenge, with some banks offering "CD promotions above 3.85%."

  • Expense pressures were noted, especially related to OREO costs and seasonal factors, with Brodkowitz describing a "bulge" in OREO expenses as more rearview mirror but some continuation expected.

  • Credit quality risks addressed through proactive monitoring, as with the hospitality loan placed in special mention.

  • Analysts expressed concern about sustainability of deposit retention and the impact of competition on pricing and margins.

Final Takeaway

Hanmi enters 2026 focused on generating low to mid-single-digit loan growth, strategically expanding deposits, maintaining strong asset quality, and practicing disciplined expense management. The company’s leadership remains confident in its relationship-based model, emphasizing a stable funding mix and proactive credit oversight, even as competitive pressures and operating expenses are monitored closely for their impact on future performance.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Archer Daniels Midland to pay $40M settlement over SEC claims of performance inflation
data.description:
The SEC said Tuesday that Archer Daniels Midland (ADM) agreed to pay $40M in a civil penalty to settle charges against the company and three former executives in a case that claimed they inflated the company's performance.

The SEC alleged ADM (ADM) and former executives Vince Macciocchi, Ray Young, and Vikram Luthar materially inflated the performance of the company's key nutrition business segment, which was touted to investors as an important driver of the company's overall growth.

Macciocchi agreed to pay disgorgement and prejudgment interest and a civil penalty totaling ~$530K, and Young agreed to pay ~$650K in interest and civil penalties.

A litigated action claimed Luthar directed "adjustments" to transactions between ADM's (ADM) nutrition segment and its other business segments when the nutrition business was falling short of its financial targets in 2021 and 2022.

ADM (ADM) launched an internal investigation and voluntarily reported its findings to SEC staff, and implemented new internal accounting controls and amended policies and procedures.

data.title: Enova expects 15% origination growth and at least 20% EPS growth in 2026 as Grasshopper acquisition advances
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Enova International, Inc. (ENVA) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Executive Chairman David Fisher highlighted the completion of a planned leadership transition, with Steven Cunningham becoming CEO and Scott Cornelis taking on the CFO role effective January 1. Fisher stated, "With the full support of the Board, this move was thoroughly, thoughtfully and deliberately planned over more than a year," and emphasized confidence in the new leadership to drive the next phase of growth.
  • CEO Steven Cunningham noted, "Our fourth quarter results capped off another exceptional year for Enova. Strong originations growth and solid credit across our portfolio once again drove strong financial performance." Cunningham reported that originations for the full year 2025 grew 27% and revenue increased nearly 20%, resulting in adjusted EPS growth of 42%.
  • Cunningham detailed that small business products represented 68% of the portfolio at year-end, with consumer products accounting for 32%. He announced the pending acquisition of Grasshopper Bank, describing it as the "perfect partner at the perfect time to take Enova to the next level by simplifying our regulatory structure, opening up additional markets for our consumer products adding additional low-cost funding sources, providing a platform for new product opportunities."
  • Cunningham announced expected annual net synergies from the Grasshopper transaction of $125 million to $220 million within two years post-closing, and projected adjusted EPS accretion of more than 25% once synergies are fully realized. The acquisition is anticipated to close in the second half of 2026.
  • CFO Scott Cornelis stated, "We're pleased to close 2025 with solid fourth quarter financial results that once again met or exceeded our expectations."

Outlook

  • Cornelis stated, "For the first quarter of 2026, we expect total company revenue to be flat to slightly higher sequentially."
  • He outlined that net revenue margin for Q1 2026 is expected to be between 55% to 60%, with marketing expense in the upper teens as a percentage of revenue.
  • For full year 2026, Cornelis said, "Assuming a stable macroeconomic environment with no material changes in the employment situation and a largely unchanged interest rate environment, we would expect growth in originations for the full year 2026 compared to the full year of 2025 to increase by around 15%." He added, "Adjusted EPS growth of at least 20%."
  • Cornelis clarified that 2026 expectations "do not assume any contribution from the pending acquisition of Grasshopper Bank."

Financial Results

  • Total company revenue for Q4 2025 was reported at $839 million, up 15% year-over-year, as combined loan and finance receivables increased 23%.
  • Q4 originations reached $2.3 billion, a 32% rise from the prior year. Small business lending revenue increased 34% year-over-year to $383 million, while consumer revenue increased 3% to $446 million.
  • Consolidated net charge-off ratio for Q4 was 8.3%, down from the prior year, and the 30-plus day delinquency ratio declined 70 basis points to 6.7%.
  • Marketing expense rose to $192 million, or 23% of revenue, compared to $151 million or 21% in the prior year quarter. Operations and technology expenses were $68 million, or 8% of revenue. General and administrative expenses included $6.7 million in one-time deal-related costs.
  • Adjusted EPS for Q4 was $3.46, a 33% increase year-over-year. Adjusted EBITDA grew 21% to $211 million.
  • Share repurchases in Q4 totaled approximately 278,000 shares at $35 million.
  • Liquidity at quarter end was $1.1 billion, including $422 million in cash and $649 million in available borrowing capacity.

Q&A

  • Moshe Orenbuch, TD Cowen, Research Division: Asked about consumer business growth and potential impacts from tax season. CEO Cunningham responded that growth accelerated in December and early January, supported by strong credit, and noted, "With the tax refund season, I mean I would -- based on what we know today, it sounds like there's potential for some larger refunds this year, which would be great for credit for us."
  • Orenbuch also inquired about the Grasshopper acquisition’s operational impact. Cunningham replied, "Until we close the transactions -- the transaction, both companies are business as usual."
  • William Ryan, Seaport Research Partners: Asked about origination growth mix for 2026. Cunningham stated, "We feel like we're in a pretty good position with what we know today to grow around 15% for the year," and expects a "slow tilt in our portfolio towards SMB."
  • Ryan followed with a question on underwriting adjustments. Cunningham explained ongoing nimbleness in credit management and said, "In consumer, in particular, after we saw sort of the exceptionally strong credit in the third quarter we did try to move back to more typical levels of credit."
  • David Scharf, Citizens JMP Securities: Asked about post-close capital ratios and buybacks. Cunningham explained, "We're sitting at around 17%, 18% tangible capital ratio... I would not expect us to be changing our leverage position dramatically."
  • Scharf asked about consumer product mix. Cunningham noted, "We're pretty agnostic across our products in terms of the growth."
  • John Hecht, Jefferies: Asked about state expansion post-Grasshopper. Cunningham confirmed intention to enter new states more easily under a national bank charter, mentioning California, Pennsylvania, and Ohio.
  • Hecht also inquired about regulatory changes. Cunningham indicated a "relatively quiet" state-level environment and described the future reporting structure post-acquisition.
  • Vincent Caintic, BTIG: Asked about potential impacts of rate caps and small business growth. Cunningham said rate caps on credit cards would "probably be a positive for us" and described small business credit as "remarkably stable."
  • Kyle Joseph, Stephens: Asked about SMB seasonality from tax refunds and scalability of the business. Cunningham stated no anticipated changes in seasonality and reaffirmed ongoing operating leverage.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed congratulations on management changes and highlighted sustained interest in growth mix, credit quality, origination trends, and the regulatory impact of the Grasshopper acquisition. The tone was generally neutral with occasional positive notes, especially regarding credit and growth prospects.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone, using language such as "we're pleased to deliver fourth quarter results that were in line or better than our expectations" and "we expect 2026 to be another year of significant origination revenue and adjusted EPS growth." During Q&A, the tone remained steady and informative, with no notable defensiveness or hesitation.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s confidence level was similarly high, with more explicit forward-looking statements tied to the Grasshopper acquisition and 2026 growth targets. Analysts in both quarters remained focused on credit quality and competitive dynamics, with slightly more attention this quarter on post-acquisition strategy.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for Q1 2026 revenue is "flat to slightly higher sequentially," while full-year 2026 originations growth is targeted at 15% and adjusted EPS growth at least 20%, compared to prior expectations centered on high single- to low double-digit revenue growth and 20%-25% adjusted EPS growth for Q4 2025.
  • The pending Grasshopper acquisition emerged as a significant new theme, with management projecting synergy-driven EPS accretion and expanded market access.
  • Analysts' questions this quarter emphasized the operational implications of the Grasshopper deal, regulatory capital, and state expansion opportunities, whereas the previous quarter focused more on marketing efficiency and credit trends.
  • Management’s tone remained consistent in expressing confidence, but this quarter included more detailed strategic outlooks and explicit synergy targets.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified regulatory approval and integration planning for the Grasshopper acquisition as a key focus, with expectations for closing in the second half of 2026.
  • Analysts raised questions about potential impacts from rate caps, tax seasonality, and post-acquisition capital allocation.
  • Management noted ongoing monitoring of credit trends and macroeconomic environment, but described the current regulatory and competitive environments as stable.

Final Takeaway

Enova International closed 2025 with strong originations and revenue growth, a successful leadership transition, and a pending acquisition of Grasshopper Bank expected to drive additional synergy and growth. Management projects 15% origination growth and at least 20% adjusted EPS growth for 2026, excluding any contributions from Grasshopper, and aims for more than 25% EPS accretion once expected synergies are realized post-acquisition. The company’s balance sheet remains strong, with ample liquidity and continued operating leverage, while analysts focused on the evolving growth mix, regulatory positioning, and strategic expansion opportunities tied to the new bank charter.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Crude oil gains with focus on supply disruptions from U.S. winter storm
data.description:
Crude oil futures turned higher Tuesday, supported by concerns over U.S. production losses brought on by winter storm Fern as well as possible supply disruptions in Iran as U.S. naval assets arrived in the Middle East.

The severe winter storm that has swept across the U.S. knocked out an estimated 2M bbl/day of domestic oil production, or ~15% of national output, over the weekend, and exports of crude oil and liquefied natural gas from U.S. Gulf Coast ports reportedly fell to zero on Sunday.

Meanwhile, a U.S. aircraft carrier and its accompanying warships have arrived in the Middle East, expanding President Trump's capabilities to potentially take military action against Iran.

"While the weather interruptions are temporary, icy wet soggy conditions across Oklahoma, Louisiana and Texas will slow the re-starts," while tensions between the U.S. and Iran as well as the lack of news on Russia-Ukraine peace efforts are "keeping a bid under crude," BOK Financial senior VP of trading Dennis Kissler said in a note.

Kazakhstan's biggest oilfield likely will restore less than half of its normal production by February 7 as it slowly recovers from a fire and power outage, Reuters reported.

Investors are also awaiting OPEC+'s scheduled meeting on Sunday, when members are expected to maintain current output levels.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for March delivery finished +2.9% to $62.39/bbl, and front-month Brent March crude (CO1:COM) closed +3% to $67.57/bbl, the highest settlement for the respective benchmarks since October 8 and September 29.

U.S. natural gas futures recovered early losses to settle at a new three-year high, as continued severe cold across much of the U.S. kept supply concerns alive; front-month Nymex natural gas (NG1:COM) for February ended +2.2% to $6.954/MMBtu.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (XLE)

data.title: Stride reaffirms $2.48B–$2.555B revenue target for FY 2026 as platform issues subside and demand remains strong
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Stride, Inc. (LRN) Q2 2026

Management View

  • CEO James Rhyu opened by stating the company has "executed on our plan and the core issues are behind us" regarding previously reported platform challenges, emphasizing ongoing improvements to the customer experience and a shift toward proprietary solutions for greater control. He expressed confidence that "we will not have a recurrence of these issues in the upcoming season."
  • Rhyu highlighted "continued strength in demand for our products and resiliency in our existing enrollments," noting that "the trends that have driven our strong enrollment growth over the last few years remain." The company focused on stability over growth this year, backfilling attrition to end the quarter flat with the prior quarter.
  • Addressing platform migration, Rhyu said, "we went to the market to get what we believe and what the market has confirmed are market-leading platforms to replace our outdated ones," but stressed the importance of retaining "a degree of influence and control over our own destiny."
  • He pointed to a "reduction in the number and types of calls to our customer support center" as evidence of improvement, mentioning that after addressing a login issue, "call volumes dropped over 90% week-over-week."
  • CFO Donna Blackman reported, "Revenue of $631.3 million, up nearly 8% from the second quarter of fiscal year 2025. Adjusted operating income of $159 million, up $23.4 million or 17% from last year; adjusted EPS of $2.50, up $0.13 from last year; adjusted EBITDA of $188.1 million, up 17% and capital expenditures of $16 million, up from $14.8 million last year."
  • Blackman also noted, "total enrollments for the second quarter were 248,500, up 7.8% from last year and up slightly from the first quarter," and that "Revenue in our Career Learning middle and high school programs grew 29% to $275.6 million, driven by enrollment growth of 17.6% year-over-year. General Education revenue declined 3.6% to $341.4 million compared to last year."
  • She stated, "Gross margins for the quarter were 41.1%, up 30 basis points from last year," attributing part of this to a gain related to exiting a noncore business.
  • Blackman announced a $500 million share repurchase authorization through October 2026, with $88.6 million repurchased in Q2.

Outlook

  • Blackman reaffirmed full-year revenue guidance of "$2.480 billion to $2.555 billion."
  • Adjusted operating income is now expected between "$485 million and $505 million, up from our prior guidance of $475 million to $500 million."
  • For Q3 2026, guidance is for revenue of "$615 million to $645 million; adjusted operating income between $130 million and $140 million and capital expenditures between $16 million and $21 million."
  • She added, "we believe we remain on track to achieve our FY 2028 financial goals."

Financial Results

  • Revenue for Q2 2026 was $631.3 million.
  • Adjusted operating income was $159 million.
  • Adjusted EPS was $2.50.
  • Adjusted EBITDA was $188.1 million.
  • Career Learning revenue was $275.6 million; General Education revenue was $341.4 million.
  • Total enrollments were 248,500.
  • Gross margin was 41.1%.
  • Capital expenditures were $16 million.
  • Free cash flow was $75.9 million.
  • Cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities totaled $676 million at quarter end.
  • $88.6 million in shares were repurchased during Q2.

Q&A

  • Alexander Paris, Barrington Research: Asked if withdrawal rates have returned to normal. Rhyu responded that "in the January month to date...we saw withdrawal rates return to normal levels, which was very good news for us."
  • Paris followed up on demand trends; Rhyu explained demand "continues to be strong. Last year was...a record-breaking year, and we're seeing volumes that are similar to last year."
  • Paris inquired about partner relations following platform issues. Rhyu described frustration among partners but stated, "a lot of our partners expressed a lot of faith in our ability to turn this around."
  • Gregory Parrish, Morgan Stanley: Questioned the cautious approach to limiting in-year enrollment growth despite stabilized platforms. Rhyu explained the company is prioritizing stability: "it's prudent, and I think our partners think it's prudent to give ourselves time to settle in here for this year."
  • Parrish asked about capacity constraints. Rhyu said, "every year...we do not have enough capacity for the demand," and that managing waitlists or deferring enrollments is typical.
  • Parrish asked about revenue per enrollment mix. Blackman explained, "the impact of our revenue per enrollment for the quarter...is really...about state mix and also about program mix."
  • Parrish requested details on a gross margin benefit from a lease exit. Blackman confirmed, "the impact on the gross margins in Q2 was roughly around 200 basis points."
  • Jason Tilchen, Canaccord Genuity: Asked about gross adds and withdrawals. Rhyu did not disclose specifics but emphasized strong demand allowed for effective backfilling.
  • Tilchen asked about platform operational performance. Rhyu said, "I feel like we're at a point where we have a good foundation now to build off of."
  • Patrick McIlwee, William Blair: Questioned risks from negative word-of-mouth. Rhyu stated, "we just haven't seen evidence that the demand characteristics are negatively impacted so far...by an overhang of sentiment."
  • McIlwee asked about G&A efficiency. Blackman attributed it to "discipline," "rightsize our adult business," and "pulled back on our marketing spend."
  • Jeffrey Silber, BMO Capital Markets: Inquired about the new partner pipeline. Rhyu described healthy ongoing discussions and no "overhang" from recent platform issues.
  • Silber asked about re-accelerating marketing spend. Blackman said, "we want to have some flexibility there...but we have some flexibility to ramp up on the marketing spend as we get to later in the fiscal year."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was positive, with congratulations on stabilization and progress. Questions focused on enrollment trends, partner confidence, and lasting impacts of past issues, indicating optimism with some cautious interest in risk factors.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone in prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently emphasizing stability, strong demand, and operational improvement. Rhyu used phrases like "I'm confident" and "we are well positioned."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone shifted from concern about platform disruptions and withdrawal rates to optimism about normalization and future growth. Management’s tone was more confident, highlighting resolved issues and the return to historical norms.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for full-year revenue was reaffirmed at $2.480 billion to $2.555 billion, unchanged from last quarter. Adjusted operating income guidance increased from $475 million–$500 million to $485 million–$505 million.
  • Enrollments increased from 247,700 in Q1 to 248,500 in Q2. Career Learning revenue and enrollments continued strong growth, while General Education revenue declined.
  • Management tone evolved from caution and focus on fixing platform issues in Q1 to greater confidence and emphasis on stability in Q2.
  • Analyst questions shifted from concerns about execution and technology implementation to inquiries about returning to growth and partner confidence.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged some ongoing work remains on platform improvements but believes "the biggest challenges to our tech implementation are behind us."
  • Blackman cautioned that platform implementation will continue to incur expenses through the year and that mix and timing of state funding, coupled with program mix, may affect revenue per enrollment.
  • Rhyu and Blackman both highlighted the need for continued discipline and careful pacing of growth to avoid repeating past disruptions.

Final Takeaway

Stride, Inc. leadership emphasized that platform challenges from earlier in the year have been largely resolved, resulting in stabilized enrollments and a return to historical withdrawal trends. The company reaffirmed its FY 2026 revenue guidance and increased its adjusted operating income outlook, supported by resilient demand, improved gross margins, and disciplined cost management. Management remains focused on stability and long-term growth, signaling confidence in meeting both near-term operational goals and its FY 2028 financial targets.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Qorvo outlines FY '27 EPS approaching $7 and gross margins above 50% amid strategic Android exit and HPA growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Qorvo (QRVO) Q3 2026

Management View

  • CEO Robert Bruggeworth stated that "Qorvo delivered solid financial performance with notable strategic achievements across each operating segment." He emphasized ongoing restructuring actions aimed at optimizing profitability and reducing capital intensity, with the closure of the Costa Rica facility ahead of schedule and the transition of production to external partners.
  • Bruggeworth reported, "At our largest customer, content gains on their ramping platform helped to support double-digit revenue growth compared to last December." However, he noted that for the upcoming fiscal year, revenue at the largest customer is expected to be approximately flat, while ultra-high-band pad revenue is expected to decline year-over-year.
  • He announced that Qorvo was awarded the high-band pad for cellular-enabled iPads, representing new content and a technology milestone. The CEO said, "The win gives us the opportunity to demonstrate capability and executed scale on that platform, consistent with our long-term investment strategy."
  • For Android, Bruggeworth indicated a strategic move to reduce exposure to low-margin mass tier smartphones, expecting Android revenue to decline by approximately $300 million in fiscal '27 versus fiscal '26. He described this as an “intentional resizing” to support higher gross margin in ACG and expanding operating margins.
  • In CSG, Bruggeworth announced initial production orders for the automotive ultra-wideband program and highlighted the successful divestiture of the MEMS-based sensing solutions business.
  • In HPA, he cited multi-year tailwinds in defense and aerospace, with sales in D&A markets expected to total approximately $500 million for fiscal year '27. He also noted strong interest in Qorvo's AESA solutions and expanding opportunities in infrastructure.
  • The CEO forecasted a mid-single-digit decline in full-year revenue for fiscal 2017, with ACG declining but becoming more profitable, CSG approximately flat, and HPA continuing double-digit growth. He projected full-year FY '27 gross margins above 50% and EPS approaching $7 per share.
  • CFO Grant Brown stated, "Qorvo's fiscal third quarter revenue of $993 million, non-GAAP gross margin of 49.1% and non-GAAP diluted earnings of $2.17 per share, all compared favorably to guidance." He highlighted a net inventory balance of $530 million, operating cash flow of $265 million, and free cash flow of $237 million for the quarter. Brown also discussed ongoing gross margin improvements and noted, "Our guidance reflects continued momentum in HPA, offset by our strategic pivot from lower-margin mass tier Android and the normal seasonal decline at our largest customer."

Outlook

  • CFO Grant Brown provided guidance for fiscal Q4, projecting revenue of $800 million, plus or minus $25 million, non-GAAP gross margin between 48% and 49%, and non-GAAP diluted EPS of $1.20 plus or minus $0.15. He forecasted non-GAAP operating expenses between $240 million and $250 million and a non-GAAP tax rate of approximately 15% for fiscal '26.
  • CEO Bruggeworth indicated that ACG revenue is expected to decline in fiscal '27 due to Android revenue reduction but anticipated a higher gross margin due to improved product mix and ongoing OpEx reduction. He projected that, as the year progresses, defense and aerospace business will be larger than Android business.
  • Management expects full-year FY '27 gross margins above 50% and EPS approaching $7 per share, citing continued operating expense discipline, improved portfolio mix, and commitment to innovation.

Financial Results

  • CFO Grant Brown reported fiscal third quarter revenue of $993 million, non-GAAP gross margin of 49.1%, and non-GAAP diluted EPS of $2.17 per share. Cash and equivalents stood at $1.3 billion, with $1.5 billion in long-term debt and a net inventory balance of $530 million.
  • Brown stated that operating cash flow for the quarter was $265 million, with $28 million in capital expenditures, resulting in free cash flow of $237 million.
  • The largest customer represented approximately 53% of revenue in the quarter.
  • Brown highlighted a sequential reduction in inventory and noted continued gross margin improvement year-over-year, attributing gains to portfolio management, divestitures, and cost controls.

Q&A

  • Thomas O'Malley, Barclays, asked about the content roadmap and competitive landscape in flagship devices. CEO Bruggeworth responded, "there continues to be opportunity for us to continue to grow our footprint there, no doubt about it," and emphasized ongoing R&D investment for their largest customer.
  • O'Malley also sought clarification on Android seasonality. Bruggeworth confirmed, "Typically, Android has been up in the March quarter... this year, it's going to be down quarter-over-quarter."
  • Peter Peng, JPMorgan, inquired about the increased pace of Android exit and longer-term revenue run rate. CFO Brown explained the shift to $300 million Android exit in fiscal '27, driven by both strategic exit and memory pricing impacts.
  • Peng asked about gross margin targets for 2027. Brown said, "the biggest driver for margin as we look out in fiscal '27 is mix... business mix as HPA becomes a larger percentage of the total, which is margin accretive as well as product mix inside of the segments."
  • Gary Mobley, Loop Capital, questioned Apple revenue assumptions and fourth quarter revenue decline. Brown confirmed the majority of the decline is Android-related, with divestitures playing a smaller role.
  • Christopher Rolland, Susquehanna, probed on integrated modules and dual sourcing. CEO Bruggeworth clarified, "the ultra-highband has been a dual sourced part for many, many years... we just have less this year than prior years."
  • Robert Mertens, TD Cowen, asked about memory pricing's effect on mobile business. Dave Fullwood, SVP, explained that memory pricing pressures mass tier Android and accelerates the strategic Android exit, but does not change the end result.
  • Edward Snyder, Charter Equity, asked about share changes, underutilization charges, and capacity utilization. Stewart and Brown confirmed no specific underutilization charges and highlighted cost management amid factory transitions.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed some concern about competitive dynamics, Android declines, and margin sustainability, with several follow-ups on business exits and mix changes. Tone was neutral to slightly negative, pressing for clarity on content, share, and margin outlook.
  • Management maintained a confident and disciplined tone in prepared remarks and the Q&A, stressing strategic focus and operational execution. CEO Bruggeworth used phrases such as "we remain confident in our highly differentiated technology" and "we expect the improvement in product mix to support a higher gross margin." CFO Brown's commentary was consistently matter-of-fact and optimistic regarding margin and cost initiatives.
  • Compared to last quarter, both management and analysts remained focused on mix shifts and Android exit, but management's tone showed heightened confidence about the structural improvements and future profitability, while analysts remained cautious but not overtly negative.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue for the third quarter declined from $1.059 billion to $993 million, while gross margin improved from 49.7% to 49.1%. Non-GAAP diluted EPS decreased from $2.22 to $2.17 per share.
  • Management increased the planned Android revenue exit for fiscal '27 from $200 million to $300 million, accelerating the business shift to higher-margin segments.
  • Guidance for the following quarter underscores a more pronounced Android decline and seasonal effects at the largest customer.
  • Analyst focus shifted more toward the pace of Android exit, margin sustainability, and capacity utilization, compared to broader product segment growth last quarter.
  • Management's confidence in achieving over 50% gross margin and EPS approaching $7 for FY '27 was more explicit than in the prior quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management outlined risks related to memory pricing and availability impacting mass tier Android build plans, but emphasized that flagship and premium tiers are not affected.
  • The strategic Android exit may create near-term headwinds for revenue but is intended to support margin improvement.
  • Divestitures, such as the MEMS-based sensing business, pose short-term revenue headwinds for CSG.
  • Analysts highlighted risks around competitive dynamics and share in flagship device content.
  • Management cited ongoing cost controls and restructuring actions as mitigation strategies.

Final Takeaway

Qorvo management highlighted a deliberate and accelerated exit from low-margin Android business, increasing the expected revenue reduction to $300 million in fiscal '27 while projecting a structurally improved margin profile, with gross margins above 50% and EPS approaching $7 per share for FY '27. The company pointed to ongoing momentum in high-performance analog, defense, and infrastructure markets, and reinforced its commitment to operational discipline and portfolio optimization, positioning Qorvo for improved profitability and a more resilient business mix going forward.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Vale's iron ore output rose 3% in 2025, topping Rio Tinto's Pilbara for first time in eight years
data.description:
Vale (VALE) reported Tuesday that its FY 2025 iron ore production rose 2.6% Y/Y to 336.1M metric tons, marking the first time since 2018 that its output surpassed rival Rio Tinto's (RIO) operations in Australia's Pilbara region.

Vale (VALE) lost the position of world's largest iron ore producer to Rio TInto (RIO) in 2019, when the collapse of a tailings dam in the Brazilian city of Brumadinho killed more than 250 people and prompted a safety review of the company's projects.

Rio (RIO) reported last week that its 2025 iron ore production from the Pilbara totaled 327.3M metric tons; when including its Canadian operations, total iron ore output hit 336.6M tons.

For Q4, Vale (VALE) said iron ore production rose 6% Y/Y to 90.4M tons, driven by Brucutu’s robust performance and the continued ramp-up of the Capanema and VGR1 projects, while Q4 pellet production fell 9% Y/Y to 8.3M tons in Q4.

Q4 copper production increased 6% to 108.1K tons, the company's highest quarterly copper output since 2018, reflecting all-time-high production at Salobo, while nickel production rose 2% to 46.2K tons.

data.title: U.S. dollar slides as markets reassess Fed path and geopolitical risks: Currency Recap
data.description:
The U.S. dollar was soft on Tuesday, extending recent losses as political uncertainty, intervention risks, and shifting rate expectations kept pressure on the greenback.

The dollar index was last down 1.30% at $95.78, and is lower by around 2% so far this year.

Dollar weekly moves and key drivers:

Over the past week, the U.S. Dollar Index (DXY), which measures the dollar against a basket of six major currencies, fell 1.78%, as markets priced in growing geopolitical risks, speculation around currency intervention and caution ahead of the Federal Reserve’s policy meeting.

 

Political developments remained a key driver, with heightened focus on U.S. trade tensions and renewed debate over dollar policy following President Donald Trump’s remarks that the currency was “doing great” despite its recent slide, while investors digested developments from Greenland-related trade concerns, ongoing tariff risks, and wider political uncertainty influencing risk sentiment. 

Analysts also pointed to rising intervention risks in USD/JPY, while Bank of America flagged increasing market preference for dollar selling amid geopolitical uncertainty.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) fell one basis point to 4.25%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) slipped one basis point to 3.59%.

Currency Highlights:

Major currency movements (January 19 to January 26)

Euro (EUR:USD) +1.78%
Pound Sterling (GBP:USD) +1.74%
Japanese Yen (JPY:USD) +2.21%
Chinese Yuan (CNY:USD) +0.01%
Swiss Franc (CHF:USD) +2.71%
Australian Dollar (AUD:USD) +2.58%
Canadian Dollar (CAD:USD) +1.07%

The Japanese yen (JPY:USD) was last up 1.29%, as markets remained alert to the risk of official intervention after last week’s sharp rally revived speculation of coordinated action by Japanese authorities, possibly with U.S. backing.

The euro (EUR:USD) was last up 1.26%, supported by improved global risk sentiment and signs of stabilizing economic activity in the euro area, as recent PMI readings showed steady composite growth and a modest pickup in manufacturing, helping European equities extend gains despite lingering geopolitical tensions.

The British pound (GBP:USD) was last up 1.13%, finding support as UK data pointed to resilient economic momentum, with January PMI readings showing broad-based expansion across manufacturing and services, alongside a stronger-than-expected rise in December retail sales. Sterling also tracked improved global risk sentiment as European equities rebounded, despite lingering uncertainty around geopolitics and trade rhetoric.

data.title: Pfizer, Novartis, Lilly drugs in latest round of Medicare drug price negotiations
data.description:
• Xeljanz (tofacitinib), Cosentyx (secukinumab), and Trulicity (dulaglutide), manufactured by, respectively, Pfizer (PFE), Novartis (NVS), and Eli Lilly (LLY), are among the 15 latest drugs chosen by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services for Medicare Part D price negotiations.
  • Other drugs are AbbVie's (ABBV) Botox (onabotulinumtoxinA), Bristol Myers Squibb's (BMY) Orencia (abatacept), and Gilead Sciences' (GILD)  Biktarvy (bictegravir/emtricitabine/tenofovir).
  • CMS said that between November 2024 and October 2025, about 1.8M individuals with Part D or Medicare Part B coverage used the 15 drugs on the list accounting for $27B in prescription drug spending.
  • The negotiated prices will go into effect in 2028.
  • The complete list of the 15 drugs is available here.
